hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_desc: Negotiate terms and report what was agreed using reported speech -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Negotiating Partnerships
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 12 -- Negotiating Partnerships

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is Anders. Quick update after my meeting. I spoke to head office this morning, and they said the fifteen percent was approved, but only if we sign before the end of the month. My director also told me that the two-year term would not be a problem, as long as the volume stays above forty room nights. Oh, and legal asked whether you could review clause seven, about cancellations. They said it needed one small change. Could you look at the draft and let me know if the terms work for you? If they do, I will send the final version to sign. Thanks a lot, and talk soon.

Welcome to this short segment on reporting a negotiation. When you agree something important, never rely on memory. Report it back, and write it down. In English, we use reported speech. Notice how the tense moves back one step. If your partner says, the rate will be fixed, you report, he said the rate would be fixed. If they say, we have approved the discount, you report, they said they had approved the discount. For questions, drop the inversion and use if or whether: they asked if we could meet halfway. Two small tips: use tell with a person, told me, told us, but use say without one, he said that. And always confirm the terms out loud before you sign. A deal that is reported clearly is a deal that gets renewed.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a formal written agreement between two sides
one specific section of a contract
to discuss in order to reach an agreement
a business relationship between two companies
to give final approval to something
the latest time by which something must be done
an early version of a document
to extend an agreement for more time
legally forcing you to do something
to compromise so both sides win a little
an advantage that gives you power in a negotiation

paras: In business, a handshake is only the beginning. Two partners can leave the same meeting smiling, each completely certain they agreed to something different. That is why the words matter -- and why professionals report and record exactly what was said before anyone signs anything.

A good negotiator listens more than they talk. They ask what the other side really needs, and then they repeat it back to confirm: 'So you said the rates would be fixed for two years, and you told me the deadline was the end of March.' This is reported speech, and it does two things at once. It shows the other side you were truly listening, and it turns a vague, friendly conversation into clear, shared terms that both sides recognise.

Then comes the draft. Every point that was agreed is written into a clause, and both sides sign off. Until it is signed, nothing is binding -- a promise is still just a promise. The best partnerships are built on this simple habit: agree clearly, report it back, put it in writing, and meet halfway when it matters. Deals made this way are the ones that get renewed, year after year.
reading.source: Adapted from a negotiation guide, Getting to Yes

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A handshake is always enough to make a business deal final.
Good negotiators confirm what was said with reported speech, write clear terms into a contract, and sign.
The best strategy in a negotiation is to talk more than the other side does.

items: The text says a handshake is only the beginning of a deal.
The first sentence says a handshake is only the beginning.
A good negotiator talks more than they listen.
It says a good negotiator listens more than they talk.
Repeating back what was said shows you were listening and clarifies the terms.
The second paragraph says it shows you were listening and turns a vague conversation into clear terms.
A promise is binding even before the contract is signed.
The text says that until it is signed, nothing is binding -- a promise is still just a promise.

parts: After the meeting, Anders called to confirm the terms. He said the discount
be approved before the end of the month. He also

gapfill.text: Reported speech: move the tense back one step (will -> would, is -> was, has -> had). Use tell + a person (told me), and say without one (said that). For reported questions, drop the inversion and use if/whether: 'She asked if we could...'.

bank.label: Useful language for negotiating and reporting a deal
items: You said the rate would be fixed for two years.
My director told me the deadline was the end of March.
They asked if we could meet halfway.
Let us meet halfway on the volume.
Once we both sign off, the deal is binding.
I will send you the first draft today.
Can we renew the partnership for two more years?
I will confirm all the terms in writing.

lf.text: Report a statement by backshifting the tense (will -> would, has -> had); use tell + person (told me); report a question with if and no inversion (asked if we could).

items.situation: A partner offers a ten percent discount, but you need fifteen.
tips: 'You said you could offer more on volume.'
Meet halfway: 'Could we agree on twelve?'

items.situation: Your director set a strict deadline that you must report to the partner.
A partner asks for more room nights than you can commit to.
tips: 'That gives you leverage with head office.'

items.situation: You need to confirm the terms before signing a binding contract.
tips: 'So, you said the rate would be fixed?'
'I will send the final draft today.'

items.situation: A partner wants to renew, but on worse terms than before.
tips: 'When we signed, you said the rate was fixed.'
'Shall I put the new terms in writing?'

items: A partner offered a discount last week. Report it back to confirm: 'You said the rate would be...'.
Your director set a condition. Report it to your partner using reported speech.
A partner asks for more volume than you can give. Offer to meet halfway.
Confirm the final terms of a partnership and explain the next step: draft, then sign off.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = would, 2 = told, 3 = term, 4 = if, 5 = clause.
Reported statement: 'He said the rate would be fixed.' (will -> would).
Reported question: 'She asked if we could meet halfway.' (drop the inversion, use if).
Use 'told me' (tell + person) but 'said that' (say, no person).
answerkey.note: Reported speech: backshift the tense one step; tell + person, say + no person; reported questions use if/whether with no inversion.
